epigraph.text: When he established the heavens, I was there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.

content.text:
The ancient Greeks discovered that many geometric figures can be created 
using only two tools: a **compass** (for drawing circles) and a 
**straightedge** (for drawing lines). These **constructions**%
 reveal the logical structure of geometry—each 
construction proves that certain figures must exist.

items: Draw a circle with a given center and radius
Transfer a distance from one location to another
Draw a straight line through two given points
Extend a line segment in either direction

content.text:
**Important:** We do not use a ruler to measure lengths. The 
straightedge has no markings.

items: Draw a ray with endpoint $C$.

Set the compass width to the length $AB$ by placing the compass 
 point on $A$ and the pencil on $B$.

Without changing the compass width, place the compass point on 
 $C$ and draw an arc intersecting the ray at point $D$.
$\overline{CD} \cong \overline{AB}$.
Draw a ray with endpoint $D$.

With center $A$, draw an arc intersecting both sides of $\angle A$ 
 at points $B$ and $C$.

With the same radius and center $D$, draw an arc intersecting 
 the ray at $E$.
Set the compass to distance $BC$.

With center $E$ and radius $BC$, draw an arc intersecting the 
 first arc at $F$.
Draw ray $\overrightarrow{DF}$. Then $\angle FDE \cong \angle A$.
With center $A$, draw an arc with radius greater than $\frac{1}{2}AB$.

With the same radius and center $B$, draw an arc intersecting 
 the first arc at two points, $C$ and $D$.
Draw $\overleftrightarrow{CD}$.

content.text:
Line $\overleftrightarrow{CD}$ is the **perpendicular bisector**%
 of $\overline{AB}$: it passes through the 
midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ and is perpendicular to it.

items:
With center $A$, draw an arc intersecting both sides of the 
 angle at points $D$ and $E$.
With center $D$, draw an arc in the interior of the angle.

With the same radius and center $E$, draw an arc intersecting 
 the previous arc at point $F$.
Draw ray $\overrightarrow{AF}$.

content.text: Ray $\overrightarrow{AF}$ is the bisector of $\angle BAC$.
Construction 5: Perpendicular Through a Point on a Line

items:
With center $P$, draw arcs intersecting $\ell$ at points $A$ and $B$ 
 equidistant from $P$.
With center $A$, draw an arc above (or below) the line.

With the same radius and center $B$, draw an arc intersecting 
 the previous arc at $Q$.
Draw $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}$.

content.text: Construction 6: Perpendicular from an External Point

items:
With center $P$, draw an arc intersecting $\ell$ at two points 
 $A$ and $B$.

With center $A$, draw an arc below $\ell$ (on the opposite side 
 from $P$).

With the same radius and center $B$, draw an arc intersecting 
 the previous arc at $Q$.
Draw $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}$.

They **prove existence**: the construction shows that the 
 desired figure actually exists.

They develop **visual intuition**: drawing constructions 
 builds geometric insight.

They connect **theory to practice**: abstract theorems 
 become tangible operations.

marginNotes.text:
I will make justice the line, and 
righteousness the plumb line.
